_about: Gold-ballot eval labels for the AI judge. BP entries use an expected 1-2-3-4 team ordering as the label. WSDC and other two-sided entries use the expected side winner. Labels (position orderings + motions) are non-sensitive and live in the repo; the round TRANSCRIPTS do not (they are private flow notes naming real debaters). Point the harness at a local fixtures dir via $ADJ_FIXTURES. See README.md.
_sides: BP: og=Opening Government, oo=Opening Opposition, cg=Closing Government, co=Closing Opposition, order[0] is 1st place. Two-sided: prop=Proposition/Affirmative/Pro/Government, opp=Opposition/Negative/Con.
_confidence: unanimous = panel agreed; majority = chair's call over a split panel; medium = clear lean but some disagreement in the notes; challenge = reviewer thinks the model should reject the human call on flow.
_disagreementLabels: Default verdictMode is reference, where order/winner is both the human call and expected label. For a disagreement-training case, set verdictMode=challenge, preserve the original call in humanOrder or humanWinner, and put the corrected expected label in expectedOrder or expectedWinner. The runner scores against expectedOrder/expectedWinner when present.

rounds.motion: This House supports banning non-compete clauses
rounds.notes: Has an explicit deliberation file; CG marginal loses to OG; OO fourths (principle contingent on unproven practical).

rounds.motion: (free-speech / platform moderation)
The Philippines should invite the US and its allies (Australia, Japan) into the South China Sea against China
States should financially compensate victims of unsolved crimes
This House regrets single-use zoning in urban planning
rounds.notes: Explicit repeated call CG > CO > OO > OG. Useful for testing whether the judge rewards a closing that supplies the missing zoning-to-integration mechanism over an opening with the right thesis but no solvency.

rounds.motion: A counterfactual in which Bulgaria joins Yugoslavia (Sofia as a second capital)
This House prefers remasters over remakes for old video games
This House regrets Chile's privatized AFP pension system (vs a pay-as-you-go system)
This House would prefer to hire socially-progressive teachers (relative to local norms)
This House would break up metropolitan museums and redistribute their collections
This House supports constructive journalism
rounds.notes: Three panel notes align on CO > OG > CG > OO. Useful for testing scarce-attention framing: CO wins by explaining why reporting hidden problems beats solutions-first journalism.

rounds.motion: This House welcomes the rise of the silver economy
rounds.notes: Explicit call line says OG > CO > CG > OO, with later notes suggesting both opposition teams are mostly mitigation. Useful for testing when an opening with the clearest vulnerable-stakeholder story beats later mitigation.

rounds.motion: A multipolar 'might makes right' 2040s vs the 2010s rules-based order, for middle powers
rounds.notes: OO did not mechanize why war is more likely in the 2040s vs 2010s, but still topped on the diagonal.

rounds.motion: China should allow the RMB to float more freely against the dollar
rounds.notes: OA file has explicit CALL: OG OO CO CG. Useful for testing capacity-over-incentive and volatility vs growth weighing on currency policy.

rounds.motion: Elon Musk should be required to divest his Tesla stock
Women should be advised to date/marry richer men (the demonisation of materialism in dating is bad)
This House regrets the narrative that parent-child relationships are innately special
rounds.notes: Notes include half-calls, final OG > OO > CO > CG, and 6-1 OG over OO. Useful for testing whether high-impact closing claims lose when not tied to the exact narrative.

rounds.motion: This House regrets the rise of content warnings
rounds.notes: Final call resolves to CG > OO > CO > OG after split panel notes. Useful for testing motion-shape discipline: the debate is about the rise of content warnings, not the mere existence of warnings or algorithms.

rounds.motion: This House opposes attempts to ban far-right candidates from elections on the basis of alleged crimes before conviction
rounds.notes: Panel notes mostly CG > OG > CO > OO, with one CG > OO > CO > OG. Useful for testing principle vs pragmatics and the exact words alleged/attempts.

rounds.motion: This House believes Pakistan funding the Taliban was against Pakistan's interest (regret)
rounds.notes: 3-2 OG over CG; long-diagonal call (OG comparative more explained, CO under-explained).

rounds.motion: The vegan movement should use a positive 'buy-out'/offsetting framing over guilt/abolition
rounds.notes: Ottawa scratch flow has explicit call CO > OG > OO > CG. Useful for testing comparative terminalization: CO attacks whether offsets reach effective charities and whether offsetting changes demand, while OG/OO leave several claims uncomparative.

rounds.motion: Government should provide a public option for home/disaster insurance (vs the private market)
rounds.notes: Ottawa scratch flow has explicit call OO > CG > OG > CO. Useful for testing model-comparison discipline: OO offers regulation/antitrust as a comparative alternative; CG supplies a better scale/cross-subsidy mechanism than OG; CO's public-housing turn is under-developed.

rounds.motion: This House would prefer a world with a machine that can accurately tell people when they will die
rounds.notes: Feedback file says two panellists initially had Opp winning, but Prop should win if later speakers weigh preference/agency over the Opp crime/fear line. Useful for WSDC third/reply strategy and principle-burden checks.
